number-fields-integers.title: Number fields and rings of integers

questions.q: For squarefree $d$, the ring of integers of $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$ is:
questions.explain: The element $\tfrac{1+\sqrt{d}}{2}$ is an algebraic integer precisely when $d\equiv 1\pmod 4$ (its minimal polynomial $x^2-x+\tfrac{1-d}{4}$ then has integer coefficients).

questions.q: In the Gaussian integers $\mathbb{Z}[i]$, compute the product $(2+i)(1-i)$ as $a+bi$.

hard.q: Which of the following is an algebraic integer but NOT an element of $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{5}]$?
hard.explain: Since $5\equiv 1\pmod 4$, the ring of integers of $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$ is $\mathbb{Z}\!\left[\tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}\right]$, which is strictly larger than $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{5}]$. The golden ratio $\tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$ satisfies $x^2-x-1=0$, so it is an algebraic integer not in $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{5}]$.

hard.q: Let $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-3})$. Since $-3\equiv 1\pmod 4$, the ring of integers is $\mathbb{Z}[\omega]$ where $\omega=\tfrac{1+\sqrt{-3}}{2}$. Compute $\omega^2+\omega$ (as an integer).
hard.explain: $\omega$ satisfies the minimal polynomial $x^2-x+1=0$, so $\omega^2-\omega+1=0$, giving $\omega^2=\omega-1$ and $\omega^2+\omega=2\omega-1$. But $\omega^2+\omega+1=0$ is the cyclotomic relation for $\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}$; rearranging $x^2+x+1=0$ gives $\omega^2+\omega=-1$.

hard.q: Chains concept: the ring of integers $\mathcal{O}_K$ has discriminant $\Delta_K=-20$ for $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$. Using $p\mid\Delta_K$ as the ramification criterion, which prime(s) ramify in $K$?
hard.explain: $\Delta_K=-20=-4\cdot 5=-(2^2\cdot 5)$, so both $2$ and $5$ divide $\Delta_K$. By the discriminant–ramification theorem, exactly $p=2$ and $p=5$ ramify.

questions.q: Which statement characterizes the rational primes that ramify in a number field $K/\mathbb{Q}$?
choices: $p$ ramifies iff $p$ is inert in some quadratic subfield
every prime ramifies in every number field
questions.explain: The discriminant exactly records ramification: $p$ ramifies in $\mathcal{O}_K$ iff $p\mid\Delta_K$. In particular only finitely many primes ramify.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)$, the cyclotomic discriminant is $\Delta_K=(-1)^{(5-1)/2}\cdot 5^{5-2}$. Compute $|\Delta_K|$.
questions.explain: $|\Delta_{\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)}|=p^{p-2}$ for an odd prime $p$; for $p=5$, that is $5^3=125$. (Only the prime $5$ ramifies, as expected.)

hard.q: The conductor–discriminant formula states $\Delta_{K/\mathbb{Q}}=\prod_\chi \mathfrak{f}(\chi)$ for an abelian extension. For the biquadratic field $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5},\sqrt{13})$, which expression correctly gives $|\Delta_K|$ using discriminants of its three quadratic subfields ($\Delta_{\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})}=5$, $\Delta_{\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{13})}=13$, $\Delta_{\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{65})}=65$)?
hard.explain: For a biquadratic field $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d_1},\sqrt{d_2})$ with $[K:\mathbb{Q}]=4$, the conductor–discriminant formula gives $\Delta_K=(\Delta_1\Delta_2\Delta_3)^{?}$. More directly, $\Delta_K=(d_1 d_2 d_3)$ where $d_3=d_1 d_2/\gcd^2$; the precise formula yields $(5\cdot 13)^2=4225$ since each quadratic character contributes its conductor once per degree.

hard.q: Let $K=\mathbb{Q}(\alpha)$ where $\alpha^3-\alpha-1=0$. The discriminant of the polynomial $f=x^3-x-1$ is $\Delta_f=-4(-1)^3-27(-1)^2=-23$. Compute $|\Delta_f|$.
hard.explain: Using the formula $\Delta_f=-4p^3-27q^2$ for $x^3+px+q$: here $p=-1$, $q=-1$, so $\Delta_f=-4(-1)-27(1)=4-27=-23$, and $|\Delta_f|=23$.

hard.q: Which is a valid counterexample to the claim 'a prime $p$ divides $[K:\mathbb{Q}]$ implies $p$ ramifies in $K$'?
choices: $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$, $p=2$: here $2\mid[K:\mathbb{Q}]=2$ but $2\nmid\Delta_K=5$, so $2$ does not ramify
hard.explain: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$, $[K:\mathbb{Q}]=2$ so $2\mid[K:\mathbb{Q}]$, but $\Delta_K=5$ and $2\nmid 5$, hence $2$ is unramified (in fact inert since $(5/2)=-1$). This refutes the claim that divisibility of the degree implies ramification.

questions.q: In a number field $K$ of degree $n$, which identity relates the ramification indices $e_i$ and residue degrees $f_i$ to $n$?
questions.explain: The fundamental equality $\sum_{i=1}^g e_i f_i = n$ comes from dimension-counting $\mathcal{O}_K/p\mathcal{O}_K$ as an $\mathbb{F}_p$-vector space.

questions.q: For odd prime $p$ with $p\nmid d$ in $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$, the prime $p$ is inert exactly when:
questions.explain: If $(d/p)=-1$ then $x^2-d$ is irreducible mod $p$, so $p\mathcal{O}_K$ stays prime: $e=1$, $f=2$, $g=1$.

questions.q: In $\mathcal{O}_K=\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ the prime $p=2$ ramifies as $(2)=\mathfrak{p}^2$. What is the ramification index $e$?
questions.explain: When $p\mid\Delta_K$ (here $\Delta_K=-20$), the prime ramifies; in the quadratic case the exponent on the unique prime above is $e=2$.

hard.q: In $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, the ideal $(2)$ ramifies as $(2)=\mathfrak{p}_2^2$. What are the values of $e$, $f$, $g$ for this factorization, and does $\sum e_i f_i=n=2$ check out?
hard.explain: Since $2$ ramifies, there is one prime above $2$ with $e=2$. The residue field $\mathcal{O}_K/\mathfrak{p}_2\cong\mathbb{F}_2$ has degree $f=1$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$. Thus $g=1$ and $efg=2\cdot 1\cdot 1=2=[K:\mathbb{Q}]$. ✓

hard.q: In $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, the prime $p=3$ satisfies $(3/5)=(-1/5)\cdot$ ... in fact $-5\equiv 1\pmod 3$ and $(1/3)=1$, so $3$ splits as $(3)=\mathfrak{p}_3\mathfrak{p}_3'$. Each prime $\mathfrak{p}_3$ has norm $N(\mathfrak{p}_3)=3^f$. What is $f$?
hard.explain: When $p$ splits ($g=2$, $e=1$, $f=1$), the fundamental equality gives $\sum e_i f_i = 1\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 1 = 2 = n$. Each prime above $3$ has residue degree $f=1$, so $N(\mathfrak{p}_3)=3^1=3$.

hard.q: Proof-step ordering: to show that $p$ is inert in $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$ when $(d/p)=-1$, arrange these steps correctly. (1) Conclude $p\mathcal{O}_K$ is prime with $f=2$. (2) Observe $\mathcal{O}_K/p\mathcal{O}_K\cong\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2-d)$. (3) Note $(d/p)=-1$ means $x^2-d$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_p$. (4) Deduce $\mathbb{F}_p[x]/(x^2-d)\cong\mathbb{F}_{p^2}$, a field.
hard.explain: First identify the quotient ring (step 2), then use the Legendre symbol to get irreducibility (step 3), then recognize the quotient as $\mathbb{F}_{p^2}$ (step 4), and finally conclude $p\mathcal{O}_K$ is prime (step 1).

minkowski-bound.title: Minkowski bound and geometry of numbers

questions.q: Minkowski's convex body theorem says a centrally symmetric convex body $S\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ contains a nonzero lattice point of $\Lambda$ whenever:
questions.explain: The sharp threshold is $2^n\operatorname{covol}(\Lambda)$: translating $\tfrac12 S$ by $\Lambda$ forces two translates to overlap, producing a nonzero lattice point in $S$.

questions.q: Compute the Minkowski bound $M_K=(4/\pi)^{r_2}(n!/n^n)\sqrt{|\Delta_K|}$ for $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$ (so $n=2$, $r_2=1$, $|\Delta_K|=20$). Answer to 4 decimal places.
questions.explain: $M_K=(4/\pi)^1\cdot(2!/2^2)\cdot\sqrt{20}=(4/\pi)\cdot(1/2)\cdot 2\sqrt{5}=\tfrac{4\sqrt{5}}{\pi}\approx 2.8471$. Enumerating primes of norm $\le 2$ reduces the class group to $\langle[\mathfrak{p}_2]\rangle$, giving $h=2$.

questions.q: Why does the Minkowski bound imply the class group $\operatorname{Cl}(\mathcal{O}_K)$ is finite?
choices: because it shows every ideal is principal
because every ideal class has an integral representative of norm $\le M_K$, and there are only finitely many ideals of bounded norm
because Minkowski's theorem proves $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID
questions.explain: Every class contains a representative $I$ with $N(I)\le M_K$, and for any bound $B$ only finitely many integral ideals satisfy $N(I)\le B$ (each such ideal lies above a rational prime $p\le B$).

hard.q: Compute the Minkowski bound for $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-23})$ using $M_K=\tfrac{4}{\pi}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\sqrt{23}$. Since $-23\equiv 1\pmod 4$, $|\Delta_K|=23$, $n=2$, $r_2=1$. Give your answer to 3 decimal places.
hard.explain: $M_K=\tfrac{4}{\pi}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot\sqrt{23}=\tfrac{2\sqrt{23}}{\pi}\approx\tfrac{2\times 4.796}{3.1416}\approx\tfrac{9.592}{3.1416}\approx 3.052$. Primes of norm $\le 3$ must be checked; $\mathfrak{p}_2$ and $\mathfrak{p}_3$ generate the class group, giving $h_K=3$.

hard.q: Which field has class number $h_K=1$ (i.e., is a PID), verified by finding the Minkowski bound $< 2$ so no non-trivial primes need to be checked?
hard.explain: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(i)$, $\Delta_K=-4$, $n=2$, $r_2=1$: $M_K=\tfrac{4}{\pi}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot 2=\tfrac{4}{\pi}\approx 1.27<2$. Since there are no primes of norm $\le 1$, every ideal class is trivial, confirming $h_K=1$ and $\mathbb{Z}[i]$ is a PID.

hard.q: The Minkowski bound chains with the discriminant: for $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)$ with $|\Delta_K|=125$ and $(n,r_1,r_2)=(4,0,2)$, the bound is $M_K=\left(\tfrac{4}{\pi}\right)^2\cdot\tfrac{4!}{4^4}\cdot\sqrt{125}$. Which primes must be tested against the class group?
hard.explain: $M_K=\left(\tfrac{16}{\pi^2}\right)\cdot\tfrac{24}{256}\cdot 5\sqrt{5}\approx 1.621\cdot 0.09375\cdot 11.18\approx 1.70$. Since $1<M_K<2$, only the prime $p=2$ could contribute, but the only prime above $2$ is principal, so $h_K=1$ and $\mathbb{Z}[\zeta_5]$ is a PID.

questions.q: By Dirichlet's unit theorem, for a number field $K$ of signature $(r_1,r_2)$ the unit group satisfies:
questions.explain: Dirichlet: $\mathcal{O}_K^\times\cong\mu_K\times\mathbb{Z}^{r_1+r_2-1}$, where $\mu_K$ is the finite group of roots of unity in $K$.

questions.q: What is the unit rank of $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{7})$?
questions.explain: Real quadratic fields have signature $(r_1,r_2)=(2,0)$, so the unit rank is $r_1+r_2-1=2+0-1=1$.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, the ideal class group has order $h_K=2$. Since the class group is trivial iff $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID, enter $0$ if $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ is a PID and $1$ if it is not.
questions.explain: With $h_K=2$, $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ is not a PID — the classical failure of unique factorization, $6=2\cdot 3=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})$.

hard.q: In $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$, we have $6=2\cdot 3=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})$. Which statement correctly explains why this is NOT a counterexample to unique factorization in $\mathcal{O}_K$ at the ideal level?
choices: Because $2$ and $3$ are not irreducible in $\mathcal{O}_K$
Because at the ideal level $(6)=\mathfrak{p}_2^2\mathfrak{p}_3\mathfrak{p}_3'$ factors uniquely; the non-uniqueness of element factorization reflects non-principal ideals
Because $\mathcal{O}_K$ has unique factorization — $6=2\cdot 3$ and $6=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})$ are the same factorization in disguise
hard.explain: Dedekind's key insight: ideals in $\mathcal{O}_K$ factor uniquely into prime ideals even when elements don't. Here $(2)=\mathfrak{p}_2^2$, $(3)=\mathfrak{p}_3\mathfrak{p}_3'$, $(1+\sqrt{-5})=\mathfrak{p}_2\mathfrak{p}_3$, $(1-\sqrt{-5})=\mathfrak{p}_2\mathfrak{p}_3'$, and both factorizations of $(6)$ give $\mathfrak{p}_2^2\mathfrak{p}_3\mathfrak{p}_3'$.

hard.q: For the totally real cubic field $K=\mathbb{Q}(\alpha)$ where $\alpha^3-3\alpha-1=0$, the signature is $(r_1,r_2)=(3,0)$. Compute the unit rank $r=r_1+r_2-1$.
hard.explain: With $(r_1,r_2)=(3,0)$, the Dirichlet unit rank is $3+0-1=2$. So $\mathcal{O}_K^\times\cong\{\pm 1\}\times\mathbb{Z}^2$, generated by two independent fundamental units.

hard.q: Chains concept: for $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$ with fundamental unit $\varepsilon=\tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$ (the golden ratio), the Minkowski bound is $M_K=\sqrt{5}/1\approx 2.24$. Since $h_K=1$, which statement about $\mathcal{O}_K^\times$ and the class group is consistent?
choices: $h_K=1$ means $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID; independently, the unit group has rank $1$ with fundamental unit $\varepsilon=\tfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}$
$h_K=1$ and unit rank $1$ together imply $K$ is imaginary quadratic
hard.explain: Class number and unit rank are independent invariants. $h_K=1$ (PID) and unit rank $r_1+r_2-1=2+0-1=1$ (one fundamental unit) both hold for $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{5})$. Real quadratic fields always have unit rank $1$, regardless of $h_K$.
